name: Feature Crutch Removal
one_liner: Remove prompting and feature crutches as models improve.
definition: A process of systematically removing workarounds and prompting interventions from a product when a new, smarter model no longer needs them.
explanation: Cat Wu describes how Claude Code initially needed a to-do list feature and constant reminders to complete all tasks. With Opus 4 and later models, the model naturally performs the full list without intervention. The team now revisits the entire system prompt with each model launch to remove unnecessary sections.
when_to_use: When upgrading to a more capable model that may not need previous hacks
To simplify product and reduce complexity over time
when_not_to_use: When the model hasn't improved enough to handle the task without support
If the crutch adds value for users beyond compensating for model weakness
metric_or_method: Subjective assessment per system prompt section: 'Does the model still need this reminder?'
common_pitfalls: Removing too early and breaking functionality
Assuming all crutches become obsolete at the same time
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
source_excerpt: We actually do this every time we launch a model, we read through the entire system prompt and we reflect on, okay, for each of these sections, does the model really need this reminder anymore, and if not, we'll remove it.
codex_prompt_template: Given a list of prompting interventions and workarounds used with an older model, evaluate each one: is it still needed with the new model? Output a list to keep or remove.

name: Formal verification for AI-generated code
one_liner: Using formal methods to mathematically prove correctness of AI-generated code.
definition: Applying formal verification techniques (e.g., compile-time guarantees like Rust's memory safety) to ensure AI-generated code is correct by construction.
explanation: Bret Taylor advocates for using programming languages with strong compile-time guarantees (like Rust) so that the act of compiling successfully proves properties like memory safety, reducing the need to read every line of AI-generated code.
when_to_use: When AI generates code at scale
When safety and correctness are critical
When you want to minimize manual code review
when_not_to_use: When the problem domain is not amenable to formal verification
When development speed is prioritized over formal guarantees
metric_or_method: Compile-time verification success rate
common_pitfalls: Overhead of learning and using formal methods
Limited scope of properties that can be verified
source_title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more
source_excerpt: you could probably layer on some techniques that are out of fashion, like formal verification, unit testing, other things. And if you layer all these on, I'm thinking about it as, I as a... It's like the guy in the matrix with the green letters coming down, how can I make something so I as a operator of the code generating machine can produce incredibly complex scale software, incredibly quickly and know that it works?
codex_prompt_template: Given the following AI-generated Rust code, verify that it compiles and has no memory safety issues: {{code}}

name: Hallucination Eval
one_liner: Detect whether the LLM accurately uses provided context or invents information.
definition: Is the agent accurately using the provided context, or is it making things up?
explanation: Crucial for document-grounded tasks. Typically implemented via LLM-as-judge, checking if the response contradicts or adds unsupported facts from the given context. Often a starting point for production evals.
when_to_use: When the system uses provided documents (PDFs, retrieval contexts) to answer
As a first eval to move beyond vibe checks
When factual accuracy is critical (e.g., medical, legal, financial)
when_not_to_use: When the task is purely creative and accuracy is irrelevant
When context is not provided (open-ended generation)
metric_or_method: LLM-as-judge classification: hallucination vs. non-hallucination
common_pitfalls: Judge LLM may itself hallucinate or be lenient
Need high-quality context and clear definition of 'made up'
Long contexts may cause recency bias in judge
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
source_excerpt: Hallucination: Is the agent accurately using the provided context, or is it making things up? Useful for: When you are providing documents (e.g. PDFs) for the agent to perform reasoning on top of
codex_prompt_template: You are evaluating whether an AI response contains hallucinations. Context: {context}. Response: {response}. Does the response contain any information not supported by the context? Answer only: Yes or No.

name: Human-analogy reasoning for model behavior
one_liner: Expect and design model behaviors by reasoning analogically from human behavior.
definition: A design approach where you predict how a model should behave by considering how a human would behave in the same situation.
explanation: Kevin gives examples: when a model needs time to think (like deep research or reasoning), design the interaction like a human would – provide brief updates rather than going silent or babbling. Also, brainstorming with multiple models mirrors human group brainstorming. This helps design intuitive UIs.
when_to_use: Designing model interaction patterns (UI/UX)
Predicting edge cases in model behavior
when_not_to_use: When model behavior is fundamentally different from human cognition
metric_or_method: User satisfaction, perceived naturalness
common_pitfalls: Over-anthropomorphizing; assuming human-like limitations or strengths
source_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)
source_excerpt: You can actually reason about it like a group of humans or an individual human and it works. [...] If you asked me something that I needed to think for 20 seconds to answer, what would I do? I wouldn't just go mute and not say anything and shut down for 20 seconds and then come back. So we shouldn't do that.
codex_prompt_template: Given a model interaction scenario, simulate how a human would behave and use that to define expected model behavior.

name: Human Evals
one_liner: Tracking win rate over successive model versions to ensure each new model improves over its predecessor.
definition: An evaluation process where, as new model versions are developed, their outputs are compared to the previous version via human raters. The goal is to maintain a consistent win rate (e.g., >50%) to confirm improvements and detect regressions.
explanation: This is part of an iterative training pipeline: after each training run, the new model is evaluated against the old one on a set of prompts. If the new model wins less than 50% of the time, it may be regressing, and training adjustments are needed. This ensures that each release is genuinely better.
when_to_use: When iterating on a model for a specific product with frequent releases.
When you need to detect subtle regressions in quality.
When you need direct user satisfaction signals
When building a labeled ground-truth dataset for automated eval validation
When tuning with RLHF
When building initial ground truth dataset
When team size is small to medium
When domain expertise is critical and concentrated in one person
when_not_to_use: When human evaluation is not feasible due to cost or scale.
When the metric is noisy and win rates fluctuate without meaningful changes.
When you need dense, frequent feedback (users rarely engage)
When you need a strong, unambiguous signal (thumbs-up is vague)
When you need scalable, low-cost evaluation at production volume
When product spans multiple complex domains with different cultural contexts (need multiple annotators)
When you have resources for structured multi-annotator processes
When you need fine-grained quality scores for ranking (though consider using a binary classifier as a component)
metric_or_method: Win rate (percentage of comparisons where new model wins over old model).
common_pitfalls: Not having enough comparisons to achieve statistical significance.
Rater fatigue or inconsistency affecting reliability.
Sparse feedback: most users don't interact with feedback widgets
Ambiguous signal: a thumbs-up may mean many things
High cost if using paid human labelers
Using multiple annotators without measuring agreement
Using Likert scales instead of binary pass/fail
Using Likert scales thinking they capture more nuance
source_title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
source_excerpt: We will take Gore human annotators, and we'll ask them, 'Okay, go have a conversational model.' And maybe you're having this conversation with the model across all of these different topics. So you are a Nobel Prize winning physicist... they are not just giving your responses, they're actually working through the responses deeply themselves... they're going to evaluate the code that it write. They're going to double check the physics equations that it writes. They're going to evaluate the models in a very deep way, so they're going to pay attention to accuracy and instruction following, all these things that casual users don't...
codex_prompt_template: Compare the following two model responses to the same prompt. Which one is better? (A) [new model response] (B) [old model response]

sources.title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
sources.excerpt: Human evals: These are human feedback loops you can design into your product (i.e. showing a thumbs-up/thumbs-down or a comment box next to an LLM response, for your user to provide feedback). You can also have human labelers (i.e. subject-matter experts) provide their labels and feedback, and use this for aligning the application with human preferences via prompt optimization or fine-tuning a model (aka reinforcement learning from human feedback, or RLHF).

sources.title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
sources.excerpt: For most small to medium-size companies, the most effective approach is to designate a single principal domain expert as the arbiter of quality. … Appointing a single expert, sometimes called a 'benevolent dictator,' provides a consistent and deeply informed signal.

sources.title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
sources.excerpt: Many teams are tempted to use a 1-to-5 Likert scale, believing it captures more nuance. This is a trap. … Binary decisions force clarity.

sources.title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
sources.excerpt: So we often have team lunches and whenever there's a new model we're testing, one of the fastest ways for us to get feedback is to just at these team lunches, just go to every single person and just be like, 'Hey, what is your vibe on the model?' [...] So that informs what data we look at to verify, okay, is this a larger pattern?

sources.title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
sources.excerpt: We will take Gore human annotators, and we'll ask them, 'Okay, go have a conversational model.' And maybe you're having this conversation with the model across all of these different topics. So you are a Nobel Prize winning physicist... they are not just giving your responses, they're actually working through the responses deeply themselves... they're going to evaluate the code that it write. They're going to double check the physics equations that it writes. They're going to evaluate the models in a very deep way, so they're going to pay attention to accuracy and instruction following, all these things that casual users don't...

sources.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
sources.excerpt: you can have specific trainers or you can have internal people to when you have a conversation of the prompt and then you have various completion of models, you choose the win rate. Which model is the best? Which model produce the highest quality comment or edit? And then you can have continuous win rates.

sources.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
sources.excerpt: as you develop new models it should always win over the previous models.

sources.title: Anthropic’s CPO on what comes next | Mike Krieger (co-founder of Instagram)
sources.excerpt: Some of it's vibes like we're still pretty early in this process and some of it is actually trying it and being one of my favorite early access quotes was the founder heard this engineer screaming next to him.

name: Inspecting Agent Tool Calls for Transparency
one_liner: Asking the agent to explain each step it took (tools, reasoning, thinking) to accomplish a task.
definition: A diagnostic practice where after an agent completes a task, the user asks it to walk through each step (e.g., read_file, search_replace, tool calls, thinking) to understand how it works, what tools it used, and why.
explanation: Coding agents transparently show their work. By inspecting the sequence of tool calls and reasoning, users can verify outputs, debug failures, and learn how agent workflows operate. This builds intuition about tool-calling patterns and the agent's decision-making.
when_to_use: After an agent executes a task, to verify it did what you expected
When debugging why an agent produced an unexpected result
As a learning tool for new users to understand agent mechanics
When evaluating which model has better tool-calling skills
when_not_to_use: For very simple tasks where the process is obvious
When the user doesn't need or want the detailed breakdown
metric_or_method: Accuracy of agent's self-report vs. actual tool call log (e.g., in Cursor's timeline).
common_pitfalls: The agent may oversimplify or miss steps in its self-report
Not all agent harnesses provide the same level of transparency
Focusing too much on the narrative rather than the actual tool call history
source_title: How to build AI product sense
source_excerpt: Now ask your agent: 'Can you walk me through each step (tool/thinking/reasoning/anything else) you used to accomplish this task?' ... In our test, our LLM reported that: 1. It used a tool called read_file to find out what was inside the file, 2. It thought about what to edit, 3. It used a tool called search_replace to modify the text file.
codex_prompt_template: You just finished [task]. Please provide a step-by-step breakdown of every tool call, reasoning step, and decision you made, in chronological order. Include the exact inputs/outputs for each tool call.

name: Latent demand discovery
one_liner: Observe what the model tries to do and build features that let it express that capability more easily.
definition: A modern twist on latent demand: instead of only watching users, also watch what the AI model naturally attempts to do during interactions, then build features that lower friction for those behaviors.
explanation: When you see the model repeatedly trying to perform actions that aren't yet supported or are cumbersome (e.g., reading an image, running a command), those represent product opportunities. This pattern flips the classic approach: rather than starting with a user need, start with the model's emerging capabilities and make them accessible.
when_to_use: When designing conversational or agentic AI interfaces
When the model's behavior reveals latent capabilities not yet exposed in the product
When you want to uncover novel product directions beyond traditional user research
When you see a growing pattern of misuse or workarounds in your product
When aiming to expand a niche tool to a broader audience
When looking for high-confidence product opportunities
When evaluating an agent's general capabilities and safety in an unconstrained setting
When the product is meant to be model-led (e.g., exposing the model directly)
when_not_to_use: When the model's hallucinations or randomness lead to irrelevant actions
When you lack instrumentation to track model-initiated behaviors
When the misuse is rare or an edge case
When the misuse indicates a security vulnerability rather than a feature gap
When a very specific, constrained output is required
When safety risks of unconstrained exploration are unacceptable
metric_or_method: Analysis of model invocation logs and user interaction traces
common_pitfalls: Over-indexing on model behavior that is actually a bug or hallucination
Building features that only a small fraction of users will trigger
Neglecting user intent and building features that users don't actually want
Confusing one-off creative uses with persistent demand
Building a product for a misuse that doesn't scale
Ignoring the original product's core mission
Interpreting random or suboptimal behavior as latent demand (confirmation bias)
Underestimating safety risks when model is given free rein
source_title: Boris Cherny
source_excerpt: Latent demand is this idea that if you build a product in a way that can be hacked, or can be misused by people in a way it wasn't really designed for to do something that they want to do, then this helps you as the product builder learn where to take the product next. [...] so, people are jumping through hoops to use a terminal to do this thing. Maybe we should just build a product for them.
codex_prompt_template: Review logs of agentic AI interactions and classify actions the model attempted but failed at or had to work around. Score each by frequency and user impact, then prioritize building direct support for the top actions.

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.excerpt: Latent demand is this idea that if you build a product in a way that can be hacked, or can be misused by people in a way it wasn't really designed for to do something that they want to do, then this helps you as the product builder learn where to take the product next. [...] so, people are jumping through hoops to use a terminal to do this thing. Maybe we should just build a product for them.

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.excerpt: The modern framing that I've been seeing in the last six months is a little bit different. And it's look at what the model is trying to do, and make that a little bit easier. [...] And so, when we first started building Claude Code, I think a lot of the way that people approached designing things with LLMs is they put the model in a box...

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.excerpt: We want to see what the model is trying to do. In product terms, latent demand is just the same exact concept but applied to a model.

name: LLM Arena Pitfalls Awareness
one_liner: Recognizing that leaderboards like LMSYS Chatbot Arena can be gamed by superficial features and don't reflect true quality.
definition: LLM Arena is a popular online leaderboard where random users vote on AI responses. It rewards flashy, verbose, and emoji-laden outputs, often at the expense of accuracy and truthfulness.
explanation: Users skim responses and pick flashier ones, even if hallucinated. This creates perverse incentives for labs to optimize for superficial appeal rather than real correctness.
when_to_use: When you need to understand the limitations of public leaderboards
When communicating to stakeholders why your model may not rank higher despite being better
when_not_to_use: When you have reliable internal human evaluations
When your target use case values accuracy over flashiness
metric_or_method: Metric: LMSYS Arena Elo score (with caveats).
common_pitfalls: Gaming by adding emojis, markdown, or verbosity
Encouraging sycophancy (e.g., 'You're absolutely right')
Correlation with real-world quality is weak
source_title: The 100-person AI lab that became Anthropic and Google's secret weapon | Edwin Chen (Surge AI)
source_excerpt: The easiest way to climb LLM Arena, it's adding crazy boating. It's doubling the number of emojis. It's tripling the length of your model responses, even if your model starts hallucinating and getting the answer completely wrong.
codex_prompt_template: Analyze the risks of using public leaderboards like LLM Arena for evaluating a chatbot. List three ways the leaderboard can be gamed and explain how each degrades actual model quality.

name: LLM-as-a-Judge
one_liner: Use an LLM to evaluate a single, narrow failure mode with a binary pass/fail output, then validate against human labels.
definition: An automated evaluation technique where you prompt an LLM to judge whether a specific failure mode (e.g., improper human handoff) is present in a trace, outputting only 'true' or 'false'. The judge prompt must be scoped to one narrow failure and yield a binary decision.
explanation: After identifying a failure mode from error analysis, you write a prompt that asks an LLM to classify if that failure occurred, returning true/false. The prompt should list clear criteria for when the failure happens. Importantly, you must validate the judge against human-labeled data using a confusion matrix (human vs. judge) to ensure alignment, not just overall agreement, because rare failures can be masked by high agreement.
when_to_use: When the failure mode is too complex for a code-based eval (e.g., subjective quality, nuanced handoff rules).
When you need to run automated evaluations on many traces (e.g., in CI, monitoring, large-scale analysis).
After error analysis has given you a specific, actionable failure mode to track.
When you need scalable, automated evaluation across many examples
When you want to evaluate subjective qualities (tone, relevance)
When you have a clear rubric expressed in natural language
When you need explanations alongside judgments for debugging
When writing any LLM-as-judge prompt from scratch
when_not_to_use: When you can write a simple code check instead (cheaper and more reliable).
For broad, multi-faceted evaluations (e.g., overall quality scoring on a 1-7 scale).
When you haven't validated the judge against human labels.
When the failure mode is extremely rare and you don't have enough labeled data to validate.
When you need deterministic, high-confidence results on a single example
When you lack a labeled dataset to validate judge performance
When you can't afford the latency/cost of LLM inference at scale
When using a pre-built evaluator (e.g., from Phoenix or Ragas)
metric_or_method: Method: LLM classification prompt. Metrics: true positive rate, false positive rate, confusion matrix alignment with human labels.
common_pitfalls: Using a multi-point scale (e.g., 1-7) instead of binary pass/fail, leading to unactionable metrics.
Not iterating on the judge prompt to reduce human-judge disagreement in the confusion matrix.
Reporting only overall agreement (e.g., 90%) without breaking down by error type.
Trusting the LLM judge's output blindly without validation.
Poorly defined criteria leading to noisy judgments
Not calibrating the judge against human ground truth
Assuming single judge judgments are reliable; need volume for statistical signal
Forgetting to use few-shot examples to ground the judge
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
source_excerpt: LLM-based evals: This technique utilizes an external LLM system (i.e. a “judge” LLM), with a prompt like the one above, to grade the output of the agent system. LLM-based evals allow you to generate classification labels in an automated way that resembles human-labeled data—without needing to have users or subject-matter experts label all of your data. [...] While individual judgments may be subjective, they become empirically useful over large datasets—if a human can grade something, so can an LLM. Production systems often use techniques like confidence scores or panels of LLM judges to increase reliability.
codex_prompt_template: You are evaluating a chatbot trace. Determine if a human handoff error occurred. Output exactly 'true' if any of the following conditions are met: [list of conditions]. Otherwise output 'false'. Trace: [trace]

sources.title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
sources.excerpt: LLM-based evals: This technique utilizes an external LLM system (i.e. a “judge” LLM), with a prompt like the one above, to grade the output of the agent system. LLM-based evals allow you to generate classification labels in an automated way that resembles human-labeled data—without needing to have users or subject-matter experts label all of your data. [...] While individual judgments may be subjective, they become empirically useful over large datasets—if a human can grade something, so can an LLM. Production systems often use techniques like confidence scores or panels of LLM judges to increase reliability.

sources.title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
sources.excerpt: Each great LLM eval contains four distinct parts: Part 1: Setting the role. [...] Part 2: Providing the context. [...] Part 3: Providing the goal. [...] Part 4: Defining the terminology and label.

sources.title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
sources.excerpt: What we have is a LLM as a judge prompt for this one specific failure. Like Shreya said, you would want to do one specific failure and you want to make it binary because we want to simplify things... So the first step is, okay, when I'm doing this judge... Now, a lot of people stop there and they say, 'Okay, I have my judge prompt. We're done. Good, let's just ship it...' Don't do that, because that's the fastest way that you can have evals that don't match what's going on... measure the agreement... Here is like a matrix of this specific judge... you need to look at each type of error.

sources.title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
sources.excerpt: It's derived from our own data, so of course, it's a product manager's expectations. What I find that a lot of people miss is they just put in what their expectations are before looking at their data, but as we look at our data, we uncover more expectations that we couldn't have dreamed up in the first place, and that ends up going into this prompt.

sources.title: Inside the expert network training every frontier AI model | Garrett Lord (Handshake CEO)
sources.excerpt: there's the thing called, which we haven't talked about here, a rubrics, and rubric models are, you can put a model in as a judge. What is a good educational design, or what's a good MRI result? ... you can build a rubric that allows a model to sit in the middle and actually auto-evaluate responses.

name: LLM-as-a-Judge Alignment
one_liner: A systematic process to ground an LLM judge’s judgment in human quality standards using a train/dev/test split and TPR/TNR metrics.
definition: A rigorous process to create a reliable LLM judge by: (1) collecting human binary labels and critiques, (2) splitting data into train/dev/test, (3) iterating on the judge prompt using dev set, (4) evaluating final performance on test set using TPR/TNR instead of accuracy.
explanation: The process ensures the LLM judge aligns with the product's quality bar. It involves creating a ground truth dataset with binary pass/fail labels from a domain expert, then building and validating the judge using a train/dev/test split. Key metrics are True Positive Rate and True Negative Rate to handle imbalanced data.
when_to_use: When you need to evaluate subjective qualities like tone, relevance, or reasoning
When you need a scalable evaluator that mimics human judgment
When you want a trusted metric for stakeholders
when_not_to_use: When the evaluation criterion is objective and can be checked with code
When you don't have a domain expert to create ground truth
metric_or_method: TPR and TNR measured together; avoid accuracy
common_pitfalls: Using accuracy on imbalanced datasets
Not holding out a test set (overfitting)
Using Likert scales instead of binary
Not including critiques in judge prompt
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: This is not about writing a clever prompt. It is about a systematic process of grounding an LLM’s judgment in your specific quality bar. … Instead of accuracy, the true positive rate (TPR) and true negative rate (TNR) measured together will tell you precisely how your judge is likely to make mistakes.
codex_prompt_template: You are an expert evaluator for an AI assistant. Determine if the following assistant response meets the quality bar. Provide a binary pass/fail and a detailed critique. Use these examples as reference: {few_shot_examples}. Input: {trace}

name: Mixed-initiative agent control evaluation
one_liner: Assess whether an AI agent allows the user to override or adjust its actions without fully disabling the agent, maintaining human control and flow.
definition: An evaluation approach for agent products that checks if the user can intervene (steer, accelerate, adjust) while the agent continues operating, rather than having a binary on/off control.
explanation: Alexander uses Tesla's self-driving as an example: the user can accelerate, turn a knob to change speed, or steer slightly without turning off self-driving. This 'mixed initiative' UX pattern is a key design goal for agents like Codex, and evaluating it means testing whether the agent keeps the user in the loop and in control while still being helpful.
when_to_use: When designing agent products that need to be used frequently throughout the day
When you want to avoid flooding users with push notifications for every agent action
When the agent is expected to take proactive actions but you want the user to maintain oversight
when_not_to_use: For simple one-shot tasks where user intervention is unnecessary
When the agent is fully autonomous in a low-stakes, isolated environment
metric_or_method: User control retention score (e.g., ability to override without full shutdown), user satisfaction with perceived control
common_pitfalls: Making the agent too passive by requiring constant confirmation
Making the agent too aggressive by not allowing easy override
Neglecting to test edge cases where user override conflicts with agent goal
source_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
source_excerpt: [...] it's really interesting to think about how to build mixed initiative software that makes you feel maximally empowered as a human, maximally in control, but yet you're getting a lot of help. And I think they did a really good job with enabling the car to drive itself, but all these different ways that you can adjust what it's doing without turning off the self-driving. So you can accelerate, it'll listen to that. You can turn a knob to change its speed. You can steer slightly. I think it's actually a masterclass in building an agent that still leaves the human in control.
codex_prompt_template: Evaluate whether the agent allows users to override or adjust its actions without fully disabling the agent. Provide a description of how the override works and any limitations.

name: Model introspection for debugging behavior
one_liner: Asking the model to explain why it made a certain decision to uncover harness or prompt issues.
definition: A debugging technique where, after observing unexpected model behavior, you prompt the model to reflect on its own reasoning to identify root causes in the system prompt, harness, or delegation logic.
explanation: If the model skips a step (e.g., doesn't verify via UI), you ask it 'why did you do that?' Its self-explanation often reveals misunderstandings in the prompt, forgotten instructions, or subagent errors. This helps you fix the harness to close the gap.
when_to_use: When the model's output or behavior is surprising or suboptimal
When you need to understand a failure mode deeply
When iterating on system prompts or agentic loops
when_not_to_use: When the model's introspection is unreliable or hallucinated
When you already have clear logging or trace data
For simple, deterministic errors with obvious causes
metric_or_method: Qualitative analysis of model's explanation to guide fixes
common_pitfalls: Over-relying on the model's introspection if it is inaccurate
Not verifying the hypothesis with concrete test cases
Using it as a substitute for proper logging and instrumentation
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
source_excerpt: So sometimes when I notice that the model does something unexpected, like for example, there's situations where the model will make a front end change and run tests, but not actually use the UI. It's actually pretty useful to ask the model to reflect on why I did this, and sometimes they'll say that, 'Hey, there was something confusing in the system prompt,' or, 'I didn't realize that the front end verification was part of this task...'
codex_prompt_template: Given the following log of the model's actions: [insert log]. Ask the model to explain its decision-making step by step. Identify any discrepancies between its intent and your expectations. Suggest harness or prompt improvements.

name: Model Maximalism
one_liner: Don't over-invest in scaffolding around model limitations; bet on rapid model improvements to solve them.
definition: A philosophy that minimizes effort building workarounds for current model weaknesses, relying on anticipated rapid improvements in future models.
explanation: Kevin argues that because models improve quickly, it's often better not to build extensive scaffolding around current limitations. Instead, focus on pushing the envelope, knowing that soon a better model will overcome those limitations.
when_to_use: When model improvement cycles are short (e.g., 2 months)
When building on the edge of model capabilities
when_not_to_use: When errors are unacceptable (e.g., safety-critical systems)
When the product requires consistent performance and cannot wait for model upgrades
metric_or_method: Time-to-value, iteration speed
common_pitfalls: Releasing a product that fails too often
Underestimating the time for model improvements
source_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)
source_excerpt: The models are not perfect. They're going to make mistakes. You could spend a lot of time building all kinds of different scaffolding around them. [...] we don't spend that much time building scaffolding around the parts that don't match that because our general mindset is in two months there's going to be a better model and it's going to blow away whatever the current set of limitations are.
codex_prompt_template: Assess whether building extensive error-handling scaffolding is justified or if we should rely on expected model improvements in the next quarter.

name: Multi-turn Conversation Eval Strategy
one_liner: First judge the entire session at the pass/fail level, then isolate root cause by testing single-turn reproduction of the failure.
definition: A diagnostic approach for multi-turn conversations: start with a session-level pass/fail judgment, and when failure occurs, test if the same failure can be reproduced in a single turn to distinguish context loss from simple knowledge gaps.
explanation: Before assuming the failure is due to conversational complexity, test the same question in isolation. If the AI still fails, it's a knowledge/retrieval issue; if it succeeds, it's a conversational memory problem. This saves debugging time.
when_to_use: When evaluating chatbots or conversational agents
When failures occur in multi-turn interactions
when_not_to_use: For single-turn interactions
When you already know the failure is due to context loss
metric_or_method: Session-level pass/fail + single-turn reproduction test
common_pitfalls: Assuming all multi-turn failures are due to dialogue complexity
Skipping session-level judgment
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: When a conversation fails, the next step is to isolate the root cause. … try to reproduce the failure in a single turn. … This diagnostic step saves significant time by distinguishing simple knowledge gaps from true conversational memory failures.
codex_prompt_template: Evaluate the following multi-turn conversation. Does the AI achieve the user's goal overall? Then, for each failure, test if the failure reproduces in a single turn. Conversation: {trace}

name: North Star Metric: Unlocking Creativity and Productivity
one_liner: Focus on qualitative user stories of unlocking creativity and productivity rather than raw engagement numbers.
definition: A north star metric defined by user-reported outcomes such as saving time, unlocking creativity, or feeling more space in life.
explanation: This qualitative, outcome-based metric aligns the product toward genuine user value rather than superficial engagement.
when_to_use: When defining long-term product vision
When engagement metrics mislead
When you have strong user research practices
when_not_to_use: When you need a simple, quantitative metric for dashboards
When you cannot collect user stories at scale
metric_or_method: Qualitative: user interviews, surveys, testimonials. Quantitative: survey scores on 'saved time' or 'creative output'.
common_pitfalls: Hard to quantify rigorously
May not be timely for daily decision-making
source_title: Anthropic’s CPO on what comes next | Mike Krieger (co-founder of Instagram)
source_excerpt: I hope that what we can do is stay focused on do we repeatedly hear from people that Claude is the way that they're unlocking their own creativity and getting things done and feeling like they now have more space in their lives for the other things.
codex_prompt_template: Conduct regular user surveys asking: 'How did Claude help you today? Did it save you time? Unlock creativity?' Aggregate responses into a qualitative north star metric.

name: Overall Correctness Eval
one_liner: Assess how well the system performs its primary goal (e.g., question-answering accuracy).
definition: How well is the system performing at its primary goal? Useful for: End-to-end effectiveness; for example, question-answering accuracy—how often is the agent actually correct at answering a question provided by a user?
explanation: A broad, end-to-end metric measuring task completion. Often uses LLM-as-judge with a rubric defining correctness for the domain. Can be applied to any task type but requires careful rubric design.
when_to_use: For evaluating overall system performance on core tasks
When you need a single quality score for stakeholders
As a high-level metric to track over time
when_not_to_use: When you need fine-grained diagnosis (prefer component-specific evals)
When the task is very subjective (e.g., creative writing)
metric_or_method: LLM-as-judge score (e.g., 1-5, pass/fail) compared to ground truth
common_pitfalls: Rubric may be too vague, leading to inconsistent scores
Single score masks different error types
Judge LLM might be overconfident or wrong
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
source_excerpt: Overall correctness: How well is the system performing at its primary goal? Useful for: End-to-end effectiveness; for example, question-answering accuracy—how often is the agent actually correct at answering a question provided by a user?
codex_prompt_template: You are a judge evaluating the correctness of an AI response. Question: {question}. Reference answer: {reference}. AI response: {response}. Is the AI response correct? Answer only: Correct or Incorrect.

name: Plan Mode Eval
one_liner: Before executing, have the model produce a plan, review it, then auto-accept execution.
definition: A two-step process where the model is instructed to create a plan (but not execute) first. The plan is reviewed by the user; if approved, the model then executes. This reduces errors and improves alignment.
explanation: In Claude Code, plan mode is activated by a simple instruction not to write any code yet. The model outputs a step-by-step plan. Once the user approves, the model executes (often with auto-accept). This pattern leverages the model's planning ability while retaining human oversight.
when_to_use: For complex tasks where the model might go off-track
When users want transparency into the model's approach
when_not_to_use: For trivial tasks where the plan is obvious
When speed is critical and the model is highly reliable
metric_or_method: Plan accuracy (whether the plan matches user intent), execution success rate after plan approval
common_pitfalls: User approving a visually convincing but flawed plan
Over-relying on plan mode for every task, slowing workflow
source_title: Boris Cherny
source_excerpt: I start almost all of my tasks in plan mode... All it is is we inject one sentence into the model's prompt to say, 'Please don't write any code yet.'... If the plan looks good then you let the model execute.
codex_prompt_template: Implement a 'plan-then-execute' evaluation: Step 1: Ask the model to produce a step-by-step plan without taking action. Step 2: Manually review the plan for correctness and safety. Step 3: If plan is approved, allow execution. Measure how often execution succeeds without further intervention.

name: Power user bias awareness
one_liner: Regularly dogfood the product as a new user to avoid over-optimizing for deep features only power users need.
definition: A self-evaluation practice where product teams deliberately create new accounts and use the product from scratch to prevent the team from focusing too heavily on advanced features that only power users care about.
explanation: Alexander notes that Codex naturally attracts power users, so the team could accidentally oversolve for that audience. He signs up for new ChatGPT Pro accounts using different emails to experience the onboarding flow as a first-time user. The key metric is D7 retention for new users.
when_to_use: When your product has a steep learning curve or power user culture
When your team consists of expert users who may lose perspective on beginners
When you want to improve activation and early retention
when_not_to_use: When you have a very simple product with no power user skew
When you have dedicated user research providing good beginner feedback
metric_or_method: Day 7 retention for new accounts, completion rate of onboarding flows, time to first 'aha' moment
common_pitfalls: Using your own team's account which already has custom settings or history
Not repeating the exercise regularly as the product evolves
Focusing only on new user experience at the expense of power user needs
source_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
source_excerpt: One of the things that I'm constantly reminding myself of is that a tool like Codex naturally is a tool that you would become a power user of. So we can accidentally spend a lot of our time thinking about features that are very deep in the user adoption journey. [...] So I think it's just critically important to go look at your D7 retention. Just go try the product, sign up from scratch again.
codex_prompt_template: Create a new account and go through the full onboarding experience. Record any friction points. Check if you would return on day 7.

name: Product-Forward Evaluation (Hat of Product)
one_liner: Evaluate AI outputs through the lens of end-user experience and product desirability, not just factual correctness.
definition: Assessing model outputs by considering whether they are actually useful or pleasant to the end user, beyond just checking for accuracy.
explanation: Look at a trace and ask: 'Would I, as a user, like this output?' or 'Is this generic and off-putting?' Often model outputs can be factually correct but product-wise poor (e.g., generic recruiting email). Fix those issues promptly.
when_to_use: When evaluating creative or persuasive content (emails, copy, recommendations)
When user satisfaction is a primary goal
During early-stage error analysis to identify non-obvious problems
when_not_to_use: When evaluating purely factual tasks like classification where correctness is the only criterion
metric_or_method: Qualitative human review with product instinct
common_pitfalls: Focusing only on technical metrics and missing user experience issues
Assuming that if the model does what it's told, it's good enough
source_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (creators of the #1 eval course)
source_excerpt: We decided to start looking at traces. We looked at a trace, the first thing I saw was this email... I said, 'You know what? I hate this email. Do you like the email, given your background?' ... That's where the fun part is, is put your product hat on and get into, is this really good?
codex_prompt_template: Review the following AI outputs and flag any that feel generic, unappealing, or would cause a user to disengage. Explain why.

name: Production Monitoring with Implicit Signals
one_liner: Using indirect user behaviors (e.g., regenerations, feature toggles) as signals to detect issues that explicit evals might miss.
definition: A complementary approach to offline evals that relies on implicit user feedback—actions that indicate dissatisfaction without explicit rating—such as regenerating an answer, switching off a feature, or ignoring suggestions. These signals help surface emerging failure modes not captured by pre‑defined evaluation data sets.
explanation: Explicit feedback (thumbs up/down) is valuable but sparse. Implicit signals provide richer, continuous monitoring: if a user regenerates an answer, that suggests the first answer was not satisfactory. In coding agents, if users disable a code review feature, that indicates a problem. These signals are used to flag traces for manual inspection, which then feed into the continuous calibration loop. This approach is especially useful for high‑throughput systems where manual review of all traces is impractical.
when_to_use: When you cannot manually review all production traces due to high volume
When explicit feedback (e.g., ratings) is low or unreliable
When you want to catch emerging error patterns that evals are not designed for
When your product has actions like 'regenerate', 'dismiss suggestion', or 'disable feature'
when_not_to_use: When user interactions are minimal or non‑existent (e.g., purely background agents)
When you need deterministic pass/fail for compliance (implicit signals are indirect)
When you lack instrumentation to capture these signals
metric_or_method: Implicit signal monitoring – examples: regeneration rate, feature disable rate, copy/paste modification ratio, abandonment after suggestion.
common_pitfalls: Assuming implicit signals perfectly correlate with quality (noise may exist)
Not distinguishing between intentional regenerations and accidental ones
Over‑rotating on a single implicit signal instead of a dashboard of signals
Ignoring explicit feedback when it is available
source_title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
source_excerpt: "For example, in ChatGPT, if you are liking the answer, you can actually give a thumbs up. Or if you don't like the answer, sometimes customers don't give you thumbs down, but actually regenerate the answer. So that is a clear indication that the initial answer that regenerator is not meeting the customer's expectation. So these are the kind of implicit signals you always need to think about."
codex_prompt_template: Instrument your application to capture the following implicit signals: (1) regeneration rate, (2) suggestion dismissal rate, (3) feature disable rate, (4) copy/paste modification ratio. Set thresholds that trigger manual review (e.g., >10% regeneration rate). Log all traces that exceed the threshold and analyze failure modes weekly.

name: Prompting as Prototyping
one_liner: Using model-generated synthetic data to train a model for new product behaviors, enabling rapid iteration without human data collection.
definition: A training approach where core product behaviors are defined, and synthetic data is generated by sampling from a model (often a more capable one like o1) to create training examples. This is used to teach the model specific actions (e.g., triggering a UI, making edits) without relying on large human-labeled datasets.
explanation: For Canvas, three core behaviors were identified: triggering, editing, and commenting. The team used o1 to generate synthetic conversations and documents, then trained the model to reproduce those behaviors. This method is scalable, cheap, and allows rapid shifting of behavior distribution based on user feedback after launch.
when_to_use: When launching a new product feature with clear behavioral specs.
When you need to iterate quickly and human data collection is too slow or expensive.
When you have a more capable model to generate quality training data.
When exploring new product ideas with uncertain requirements.
When you need to quickly demonstrate feasibility to stakeholders.
When the behavior can be expressed in natural language instructions.
when_not_to_use: When the task requires rare or highly specialized knowledge that models cannot generate reliably.
When synthetic data may introduce biases or artifacts that hurt generalization.
When the behavior requires fine-grained control or high reliability that prompting cannot guarantee.
When you already have a clear spec and deterministic eval.
metric_or_method: Eval-driven training: use evals to measure performance and guide synthetic data generation.
common_pitfalls: Synthetic data lacking diversity, leading to poor generalization.
Training on synthetic data that does not match real user distribution after launch.
Confusing a prompt-based prototype with production-ready performance.
Over-relying on prompting without considering training needs for robustness.
source_title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
source_excerpt: the way you synthetically train the model is physically figuring out what are the most core behaviors that you wanted the product feature to do. And for Canvas, for example, it came down to three main behaviors... we would use o1 model to seem a way of user conversation... and then we taught the model to make comments on the document...
codex_prompt_template: Generate synthetic training data for the behavior: [describe behavior, e.g., 'model should edit the second paragraph of a document to a friendly tone']. Provide input-output pairs.

sources.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
sources.excerpt: prompting is also a way to prototype new product ideas. Early days at Anthropic when I was working file uploads feature, I remember I was just prompting the model to just... I remember we were launching a hundred key contexts. I was just prototyping this in their local browser.

sources.title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
sources.excerpt: the way you synthetically train the model is physically figuring out what are the most core behaviors that you wanted the product feature to do. And for Canvas, for example, it came down to three main behaviors... we would use o1 model to seem a way of user conversation... and then we taught the model to make comments on the document...

name: Qualitative saturation user interviews
one_liner: Stop user interviews when you can predict what the next interviewee will say.
definition: Continue talking to users until no new insights emerge, then stop.
explanation: A heuristic for qualitative research: conduct user interviews until the responses become predictable, indicating you've reached saturation and have a comprehensive understanding of user needs.
when_to_use: When exploring a new product or use case
When you need to deeply understand user behavior
when_not_to_use: When quantitative data alone is sufficient
When time constraints prevent in-depth interviews
metric_or_method: Saturation point (when new interviews yield no new insights)
common_pitfalls: Stopping too early due to bias
Confirmation bias from interview selection
source_title: Inside ChatGPT: The fastest-growing product in history | Nick Turley (Head of ChatGPT at OpenAI)
source_excerpt: ...it was just 15 minute user interview the whole week through... I stopped doing interviews when I can predict what the next person's going to say. That's how I know I've talked to enough users...
codex_prompt_template: Conduct user interviews until responses become repetitive. Log each new insight and stop when no new themes emerge for 3 consecutive interviews.

name: Quality-Volume-Speed Eval Framework
one_liner: Balance three critical axes—quality, volume, speed—when evaluating data for model training.
definition: A tripartite framework stating that model builders care about three things in training data: high quality, sufficient volume, and fast turnaround.
explanation: Quality ensures the data doesn't mislead the model; volume provides enough examples for generalization; speed allows rapid iteration of experiments. All three must be considered when creating and evaluating training data pipelines.
when_to_use: When planning data collection for model training.
When evaluating the effectiveness of a data pipeline.
When communicating priorities between data providers and model builders.
when_not_to_use: When other constraints (e.g., cost, privacy) dominate.
In contexts where evaluation is purely about model performance on fixed benchmarks.
metric_or_method: Quality metrics (e.g., expert review pass rate), volume (number of samples), speed (time from request to delivery).
common_pitfalls: Trading off quality for speed or volume.
Underestimating the difficulty of achieving all three simultaneously.
Ignoring the diminishing returns of volume past a threshold.
source_title: Inside the expert network training every frontier AI model | Garrett Lord (Handshake CEO)
source_excerpt: they really care about three things. They care about quality first and foremost. ... the other huge problems you have is volume. ... And then, the other thing I would say model builders care about is speed.
codex_prompt_template: For the given evaluation task, assess the data along three axes: quality, volume, and speed. Assign a score for each and provide recommendations.

name: Quickly saturated benchmarks
one_liner: Benchmarks get saturated quickly, requiring new frontier evals.
definition: The observation that existing benchmarks become saturated as models improve, necessitating the creation of new, more challenging evaluations to measure progress.
explanation: Karina notes that benchmarks were quickly saturated at the time of her blog post, implying that as models master a set of tasks, those benchmarks no longer distinguish between model capabilities. This drives the need for continuous development of frontier evals.
when_to_use: When tracking progress of rapidly improving AI models
When current evaluation metrics no longer show meaningful differences between models
when_not_to_use: When benchmarks are still challenging and not saturated
For stable or slowly improving tasks
metric_or_method: Process: regularly update evaluation benchmarks.
common_pitfalls: Continuing to use saturated benchmarks that give false positive progress signals
Not investing in creating harder evaluations in time
source_title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen (Research at OpenAI, ex-Anthropic)
source_excerpt: I had a blog post about this. Maybe I should update on latest benchmarks, because at that time everybody was doing one benchmark and they'd be... quickly saturated the benchmarks. So I'm like, "Now we need to do the same plot but with another frontier eval."
codex_prompt_template: Generate frontier eval that is not saturated by current SOTA models.

name: Race to the Top Eval
one_liner: Open-source safety tools and share findings to raise industry-wide safety standards.
definition: An open-science approach to evaluation and safety where a lab publishes tools, sandboxes, and insights to help all players improve safety. The idea is that rising tide lifts all boats, and competition should focus on safety as well as capability.
explanation: Instead of keeping evaluation methods proprietary, the lab releases open-source sandboxes (e.g., a safe agent sandbox) that work with any model, not just their own. They also publish research on interpretability and safety findings to 'race to the top' collectively.
when_to_use: When aiming to improve industry-wide safety standards
When building evaluation infrastructure that can be shared
when_not_to_use: When proprietary advantage heavily outweighs collaboration benefits
When the organization cannot afford to share IP
metric_or_method: Community adoption of open-source tooling, reduction in safety incidents across labs
common_pitfalls: Sharing too early before internal validation (could be exploited)
Assuming others will reciprocate
source_title: Boris Cherny
source_excerpt: We released an open source sandbox... we made it open source, and it actually works with any agent, not just Claude Code, because we wanted to make it really easy for others to do the same thing.
codex_prompt_template: Design an evaluation framework that can be shared openly. Publish a sandbox environment with monitoring, and invite the community to use it with any agent. Track safety metrics across different models and share aggregate findings.

name: RAG Evaluation: Retriever then Generator
one_liner: Separately evaluate retriever (recall@k) and generator (faithfulness and answer relevance) in a RAG pipeline.
definition: A two-part evaluation framework for RAG: first evaluate the retriever using recall@k (and precision@k if a re-ranker exists), then evaluate the generator on faithfulness to context and answer relevance.
explanation: RAG systems have two independent components that can fail. End-to-end scores don't pinpoint the issue. Retriever evaluation uses recall@k to ensure relevant documents are found; generator evaluation checks if the answer is faithful to the context and relevant to the query. Fix retriever first.
when_to_use: When evaluating any RAG pipeline
When you need to debug whether failures are retrieval or generation issues
when_not_to_use: When the system is not RAG-based
metric_or_method: Recall@k, precision@k (retriever); faithfulness and answer relevance (generator)
common_pitfalls: Only using end-to-end correctness scores
Not tuning k based on query complexity
Evaluating generator before fixing retriever
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: A RAG system is a two-part machine: a retriever finds information, and a generator writes an answer … You must evaluate them separately. … The most critical metric for RAG is often recall@k. … Fix your retriever first.
codex_prompt_template: For the given query, evaluate the retriever: does it return the ground truth document within top k? Then evaluate the generator: is the answer faithful to the context and relevant to the query? Query: {query}, Retrieved: {docs}, Answer: {answer}

name: Research directly integrated into product teams
one_liner: Embed researchers or ML engineers directly into product teams to enable fine-tuning and model customization.
definition: A team structure where ML/research talent is part of product teams to facilitate fine-tuning and custom model development for product needs.
explanation: Kevin predicts that as fine-tuning becomes standard, product teams will include ML engineers or researchers who can fine-tune models specifically for the product's use cases. This allows teams to customize models beyond using generic APIs.
when_to_use: When building products that benefit from domain-specific model performance
When you need to differentiate through model behavior
when_not_to_use: When fine-tuning isn't needed or base model performance is sufficient
metric_or_method: Model performance lift after fine-tuning, team velocity
common_pitfalls: Over-investing in fine-tuning when a simple prompt works
source_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)
source_excerpt: I think you're going to want sort of quasi researcher machine learning engineer types as part of pretty much every team because fine-tuning a model is just going to be part of the core workflow for building most products.
codex_prompt_template: Propose how to integrate an ML engineer into a product team to support fine-tuning, including roles and responsibilities.

name: Research Preview Shipping
one_liner: Ship features quickly as research previews to get early feedback and iterate.
definition: A shipping strategy where almost all features are released as research previews, clearly branded as early and unsupported, to reduce commitment and enable fast iteration.
explanation: By shipping features as research previews, the team reduces the commitment to support them forever and can get feedback and iterate quickly. This allows features to go from idea to user hands in a week or two.
when_to_use: When you want to test ideas quickly with real users
When the product is evolving rapidly and you need fast feedback loops
When you can clearly communicate that the feature is experimental
When model capabilities are rapidly improving and uncertain
To gather real-world feedback quickly
For products where being first to market is advantageous
when_not_to_use: When the feature is critical for enterprise stability or compliance
When users expect a polished, fully supported experience
When the feature requires extensive documentation and training upfront
When errors have high safety or cost consequences
When you need a polished, fully-reliable product from day one
metric_or_method: Iteration speed (time from idea to user hands)
common_pitfalls: Users may be confused about which features are stable vs experimental
Overlapping features can confuse new users
Hard to maintain product consistency across many research previews
Rushing a product that is not ready for critical use cases
Ignoring serious errors in early versions
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
source_excerpt: For Claude Code, what we do is we actually ship almost all of our features in research preview. We clearly brand this when we ship something so that users know that this is an early product, this is just an idea, this is just something that we're trying to get feedback on and iterating on, and that this might not be supported forever. And what this does is it reduces our commitment for shipping something. We can just get something out in a week or two.
codex_prompt_template: Given a new feature idea for an AI product, outline a research preview shipping plan that includes branding as early, expected iteration cycle, and criteria for full release.

sources.title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
sources.excerpt: For Claude Code, what we do is we actually ship almost all of our features in research preview. We clearly brand this when we ship something so that users know that this is an early product, this is just an idea, this is just something that we're trying to get feedback on and iterating on, and that this might not be supported forever. And what this does is it reduces our commitment for shipping something. We can just get something out in a week or two.

sources.title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil (CPO at OpenAI, ex-Instagram, Twitter)
sources.excerpt: So we have this philosophy, we call iterative deployment, and the idea is we're all learning about these models together. [...] it's way better to ship something even when you don't know the full set of capabilities and iterate together in public. [...] And we co-evolve together with the rest of society as we learn about these things and where they're different and where they're good and bad and weird.

name: Resolution-based pricing
one_liner: Pricing AI agents based on successful task completion (e.g., each resolved customer issue) rather than on usage or tokens.
definition: A pricing model where the customer pays per successful outcome (e.g., a customer issue resolved by an AI agent) at a pre-negotiated rate. This aligns the vendor's incentives with the customer's value.
explanation: In contrast to per-token or per-usage pricing, resolution-based pricing charges for measurable business outcomes. For example, an AI customer service agent is paid per resolved ticket, with resolution defined as the customer's problem being solved without escalation. This model forces the vendor to focus on efficacy and value delivery.
when_to_use: When the AI agent operates autonomously and outcomes are clearly measurable
When the value to the customer is directly tied to specific outcomes (e.g., cost savings per call)
When you want to build a partnership-oriented customer relationship
When you can accurately track and verify outcomes
when_not_to_use: When outcomes are subjective or hard to measure
When the agent requires significant human supervision (outcome attribution unclear)
When the customer is not comfortable with variable costs
For early-stage products still improving accuracy (risk of underpayment)
metric_or_method: Resolution rate (percentage of interactions fully automated); cost per resolved issue
common_pitfalls: Defining 'resolution' too loosely, leading to disputes
Not accounting for partial resolutions or transfers
Setting the per-resolution rate too low to cover costs
Lack of proper telemetry to verify outcomes
source_title: He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more
source_excerpt: So if an AI agent can take that call and solve it, that is in the industry often called a call deflection or a containment. And that essentially means you saved, call it $15 because you didn't have to have someone pick up the phone. So in our industry, basically we say, 'Hey, if the AI agent solves the customer's problem, they're happy with it and you didn't have to pick up the phone,' there's a pre-negotiated rate for that and we call it resolution based.
codex_prompt_template: Design a resolution-based pricing model for an AI customer service agent. Define what constitutes a resolution, how it will be verified (e.g., customer feedback, escalation records), and determine a per-resolution price that reflects the value saved (e.g., average call center cost per issue). Include mechanisms for handling partial resolutions and disputes.

name: RL environment eval
one_liner: Training AI agents in sandboxed environments that simulate real-world software systems.
definition: A training approach where AI agents are put into simulated environments (e.g., a Salesforce instance) to learn how to accomplish tasks, with the goal of generalizing across many similar environments.
explanation: Agents are trained in RL environments that represent real systems. They learn to navigate configurations, data types, and business processes. The key is collecting data that generalizes across many permutations of environments.
when_to_use: When building enterprise agents that interact with complex software.
When reliability and generalizability across use cases are critical.
When you need to evaluate agentic behavior over long horizons
When real-world complexity is missing from current benchmarks
To train models on end-to-end tasks with feedback
When building autonomous coding agents
When you have a clear pass/fail criterion (e.g., test cases)
When you want continuous improvement via self-play
when_not_to_use: For simple, deterministic tasks where rules can be hardcoded.
When the environment is static and well-defined; supervised fine-tuning may suffice.
For simple, single-step classification tasks
When you lack the resources to build a full simulation
For subjective tasks without clear evaluation criteria
When execution environment is expensive or risky
metric_or_method: Success rate in achieving goals within the environment; generalizability across unseen environments.
common_pitfalls: Overfitting to specific environments.
High cost of constructing realistic environments.
Difficulty in measuring true generalizability.
Ignoring intermediate trajectory (reward hacking)
Environments that are too narrow or contrived
Overfitting to known test cases
Requires robust sandboxed execution environment
source_title: First interview with Scale AI’s CEO: $14B Meta deal, what’s working in enterprise AI, and what frontier labs are building next | Jason Droege
source_excerpt: We give them models tasks in these environments, we design interesting challenges for them, and then we run them to see how they perform. And then we teach them, we give them these rewards when they're doing a good job or a bad job. [...] You have all these models that seem really smart on isolated benchmarks, they're good at single step tool calling. They're good at single step instruction following. But suddenly you dump them into these messy worlds where you have confusing Slack messages and tools they've never seen before, and they need to perform right actions and modify the [inaudible 00:36:06] and interact over longer time horizons where what they do in step one affects what they do in step 50.
codex_prompt_template: Simulate a Salesforce environment. Task: 'Book a meeting with a prospect.' The agent should explore, take actions, and receive feedback. Collect trajectories for training.

And then we teach them, we give them these rewards when they're doing a good job or a bad job. [...] You have all these models that seem really smart on isolated benchmarks, they're good at single step tool calling. They're good at single step instruction following.
