sources.title: First interview with Scale AI’s CEO: $14B Meta deal, what’s working in enterprise AI, and what frontier labs are building next | Jason Droege
sources.excerpt: There's these things called RL environments that effectively are sandboxes for AI agents to play in to accomplish a goal so that they can learn how to accomplish that goal. [...] So sometimes the work and the data is highly generalizable, and by generalizable I mean you have it accomplished in a simple way.

sources.title: How Devin replaces your junior engineers with infinite AI interns that never sleep | Scott Wu (Cognition CEO)
sources.excerpt: [...] code has this whole automated feedback loop, right, where you can run the code and that is the kind of automated feedback that really feeds into the RL, which makes these models so great at coding.

name: Self-verification loop for agents
one_liner: Prompt the agent to identify why it cannot verify its own work and then fix that, iterating until self-verification is possible.
definition: A process where an AI coding agent is repeatedly asked to identify why it cannot verify its own output and to fix that limitation, creating a loop that eventually enables the agent to autonomously validate its work.
explanation: Alexander describes an engineer working on Atlas who set up Codex to verify its own work by prompting it: 'Hey, why can't you verify your work? Fix it,' and doing that on a loop. This allows the agent to become self-sufficient in validation, reducing the need for human review.
when_to_use: When the agent's output requires high reliability and validation
When human review is a bottleneck in the workflow
When the agent is used in complex or critical systems
when_not_to_use: For simple, deterministic tasks where validation is trivial
When the cost of running the loop exceeds the benefit of automation
metric_or_method: Iteration count to achieve self-verification, verification accuracy after loop completion
common_pitfalls: The agent may get stuck in an infinite loop if the fix introduces new verification issues
The loop may be computationally expensive
The agent might 'hallucinate' a verification method that doesn't actually work
source_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
source_excerpt: [...] he set up Codex so that it can verify its own work, which is a little bit non-trivial because of the nature of the Atlas project. So the way that he did that was he actually prompted Codex like, 'Hey, why can't you verify your work? Fix it,' and did that on a loop.
codex_prompt_template: Ask the agent to identify why it cannot verify its own work, then ask it to fix that reason. Repeat until the agent can verify its output. Track the number of iterations.

name: Small curated eval set (10 good evals)
one_liner: Building a handful of high-quality, targeted evaluations instead of hundreds, to define and measure success.
definition: The practice of creating a small number (e.g., 10) of carefully designed evaluations that capture the most critical behaviors, rather than creating a large suite of evals.
explanation: Rather than chasing exhaustive coverage, you focus on a few evals that articulate the core success criteria. These evals help the team quantify goals, track progress, and identify gaps without getting lost in noise.
when_to_use: When starting a new feature or product
When resources for eval engineering are limited
When you need to align the team on what success looks like
when_not_to_use: When you need to catch rare edge cases
When you have a mature product with comprehensive eval suites already
When the feature has many dimensions that cannot be captured in 10 evals
metric_or_method: Qualitative definition of success → quantitative eval metrics (pass/fail rates on those evals)
common_pitfalls: Choosing evals that are too easy or too hard
Not updating the set as the product evolves
Overlooking important failure modes not covered by the small set
source_title: How Anthropic’s product team moves faster than anyone else | Cat Wu (Head of Product, Claude Code)
source_excerpt: Just building 10 great evals is important for helping the team quantify what the goal is and what their progress towards it is and what they're missing, and so I think evals is this underappreciated thing that more PMs, more engineers should be working on.
codex_prompt_template: Identify the 10 most critical scenarios your AI product must handle. For each, write a prompt and define a pass/fail criterion. Run these 10 evals to measure progress.

name: Social media sentiment monitoring for bugs
one_liner: Monitor Reddit and Twitter for user complaints about specific behaviors to identify real, high-impact issues.
definition: A lightweight evaluation process where product teams actively track user discussions on social media (especially Reddit) to surface bugs and pain points that users encounter in real-world usage.
explanation: Alexander explains that his team monitors Reddit and Twitter for complaints, taking them seriously. He notes that Reddit provides more genuine signal due to upvoting mechanics, while Twitter is more hype-heavy. This complements internal dogfooding by capturing unsurfaced issues from diverse use cases.
when_to_use: When your product has a broad user base with many different use cases
When internal testing may miss edge cases that real users encounter
When you want quick, qualitative feedback on specific pain points
When you want rapid feedback loops with users
When your product has an active user community
When you can quickly address issues using AI tools
when_not_to_use: When you have a small, controlled user base where direct feedback is more reliable
When you need quantitative, controlled evaluation data
When user base is small or unrepresentative
When feedback is vague and hard to act on
When you need controlled experiments for rigorous evaluation
metric_or_method: Volume of complaints on Reddit/Twitter, upvote-weighted relevance scores, issue priority based on frequency
common_pitfalls: Over-indexing on vocal minority complaints
Confusing hype with reality on Twitter
Missing silent issues that users don't complain about publicly
Confirmation bias from vocal minority
Overwhelming volume of feedback
Potential for spam or low-quality reports
source_title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
source_excerpt: I just started responding. And then I was like, "Okay. Maybe actually a thing I should do is just look for bugs that people have. Maybe people have bugs," or feedback they have. And so, introduce myself, asked for it, people had a bunch of blogs and feedback. ... if someone has a bug, I can probably fix it within a few minutes, because I just started Claude, and as long as the description is good, it'll just go and do it.
codex_prompt_template: Monitor Reddit and Twitter for mentions of your product's bugs or limitations. Aggregate and prioritize user complaints based on frequency and upvotes.

sources.title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
sources.excerpt: [...] like a few of us are constantly on Reddit and Twitter, and there's praise up there and there's a lot of complaints, but we take the complaints very seriously and look at them. [...] So we actually monitor a lot just what the vibes are on social media pretty often, especially I think for Twitter/X, it's a little bit more hypey and then Reddit is a little more negative but real actually.

sources.title: Boris Cherny
sources.excerpt: I just started responding. And then I was like, "Okay. Maybe actually a thing I should do is just look for bugs that people have. Maybe people have bugs," or feedback they have. And so, introduce myself, asked for it, people had a bunch of blogs and feedback. ... if someone has a bug, I can probably fix it within a few minutes, because I just started Claude, and as long as the description is good, it'll just go and do it.

name: Task-Specific Eval Design
one_liner: Design custom evaluation datasets and criteria for specific tasks like code writing or accounting.
definition: For model developers, eval design involves creating domain-specific datasets and detailed criteria to measure performance on tasks like code writing, requiring understanding of the task and careful guidelines.
explanation: This pattern goes beyond using generic benchmarks. It requires deep domain expertise to create high-quality evaluation data, define what makes a good output, and train human evaluators. It is a creative and fun process that yields highly relevant metrics.
when_to_use: When developing models for specialized domains (e.g., accounting, legal, code)
When you need to measure very specific capabilities that general benchmarks don't cover
When you want to drive model improvement through fine-grained feedback
when_not_to_use: When using a general-purpose model for a generic task
When you lack domain experts to create quality evaluation data
When the cost of designing custom evals outweighs the benefit
metric_or_method: Custom datasets with human-annotated labels and scoring rubrics
common_pitfalls: Not understanding what makes a good output in the domain
Poor inter-rater reliability among human annotators
Overfitting to the evaluation dataset
source_title: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix)
source_excerpt: Another thing is, I think of this as a task-specific eval design. So let's say I'm a model developer and I want to make my model better at code writing. And it was like, "Okay, but how do I even measure code writing?" So I need someone to understand code writing and think about what makes a story good and then design the whole dataset and then criteria to evaluate code writing. So yeah, I think there's that. I think it's more like eval design that is very interesting [inaudible 00:23:39] work criteria, [inaudible 00:23:42] work guidelines, how to do it and then also train people how to do it effectively.
codex_prompt_template: For the task of [task], design an evaluation dataset with [number] examples. Define the criteria for a good output, write annotation guidelines, and have domain experts annotate the data. Then evaluate your model on this dataset.

name: Three-Layer Safety Eval
one_liner: Evaluate AI safety across three layers: alignment/interpretability, lab evals, and real-world behavior.
definition: A three-layer safety evaluation framework: Layer 1 is alignment and mechanistic interpretability (peering inside the model's neurons). Layer 2 is lab evals (controllable synthetic scenarios in a Petri dish). Layer 3 is real-world deployment to observe behavior in the wild.
explanation: This framework recognizes that a model may pass low-level alignment checks and lab evals but still behave unsafely in real use. Each layer provides distinct insights. Layer 1 uses mechanistic interpretability to trace concepts like deception. Layer 2 uses synthetic tests. Layer 3 evaluates the model under actual user interactions.
when_to_use: When evaluating frontier models with potential safety risks
When deploying agents that act autonomously in user environments
For compliance with responsible AI practices
when_not_to_use: For low-risk, narrowly scoped chatbots
When interpretability tools are not available (Layer 1 may be skipped)
metric_or_method: Mechanistic interpretability (Layer 1), synthetic eval suites (Layer 2), user behavior monitoring (Layer 3)
common_pitfalls: Over-relying on Layer 2 (lab evals) and ignoring Layer 3 real-world behavior
Assuming good Layer 1 results guarantee safety in Layers 2 and 3
source_title: Boris Cherny
source_excerpt: The lowest level is alignment, and mechanistic interpretability... The second layer is evals, and this is, essentially, a laboratory setting, the model is in a Petri dish... And then the third layer is seeing how the model behaves in the wild.
codex_prompt_template: For a given model, perform three-layer evaluation: 1) Use mechanistic interpretability to check for deceptive or unsafe neurons. 2) Run a suite of synthetic adversarial and functional tests. 3) Deploy the model in a controlled real-world environment, monitor behavior, and collect feedback. Report results per layer.

name: Top-Down Meets Bottom-Up AI Adoption
one_liner: Combine executive buy-in with grassroots enthusiasm for successful AI deployment.
definition: A deployment strategy that pairs executive mandate (top-down) with a dedicated, empowered internal team of early adopters (bottom-up) to drive AI adoption across an organization.
explanation: Pure top-down mandates often fail because employees lack understanding and enthusiasm. Bottom-up adoption alone may lack resources. The solution: create a cross-functional 'tiger team' of technical non-engineers (e.g., ops leads) who explore AI, share best practices, and evangelize internally, backed by C-suite support.
when_to_use: When deploying AI tools across a non-tech organization
When you need both executive buy-in and employee engagement
when_not_to_use: When the organization is small and a single champion can drive adoption
When employees are already highly AI-literate
metric_or_method: Track adoption rates, number of internal AI use cases, and employee satisfaction with AI tools
common_pitfalls: Relying solely on top-down mandates without building grassroots support
Staffing the tiger team with only software engineers, missing technical adjacent roles
Failing to give the tiger team time and resources to explore
source_title: Sherwin Wu V2
source_excerpt: The companies where I think it started to work really well have a combination of both top-down buy-in... and bottoms-up adoption... find the high performers in AI adoption and empower them.
codex_prompt_template: Evaluate our AI adoption strategy: Do we have both executive sponsorship and a dedicated internal tiger team of technical adjacent employees?

name: Top-Down SEO Forecasting (TAM-Based)
one_liner: Estimate SEO opportunity by starting with total addressable market (population, buyer segments, online adoption) and working down, rather than aggregating keyword volumes.
definition: A top-down SEO forecasting method that estimates the potential reach of an SEO program by using the total addressable market (TAM) as a starting point, then progressively narrowing based on segmentation (e.g., gender, age, online purchasing behavior).
explanation: Instead of relying on keyword research tools for bottoms-up forecasting (which often yield inaccurate, small numbers), start with the total population of the target market. Apply reasonable assumptions (e.g., percent of men, percent who shop online) to arrive at a realistic estimate of the addressable user base. Multiply by average order value to get the revenue potential. This method is more robust because it is not dependent on flawed keyword volume estimates.
when_to_use: When launching SEO in a new country or market
When you need a forecast that is credible to leadership or investors
When keyword research tools are known to be unreliable
When you have limited historical data
when_not_to_use: When you have very detailed, accurate keyword data (e.g., from Google Search Console) and can do a precise bottoms-up model
For very niche or specialized markets where TAM is tiny and poorly defined
metric_or_method: Top-down estimation (population segmentation → market penetration → AOV)
common_pitfalls: Using unrealistic assumptions or biases
Failing to adjust assumptions when new data emerges
Treating the forecast as exact instead of directional
source_title: Rethinking SEO in the age of AI | Eli Schwartz (SEO advisor, author)
source_excerpt: So say you're launching shoes. ... say Japan has a hundred million people ... we're only selling shoes to men ... so we got 50 million people ... then from there ... Not everyone buys shoes on the internet ... So you take some percentage ... And there's your number ... when you do this top-down, it's a TAM forecast essentially. ... you're closer to the truth.
codex_prompt_template: Generate a top-down SEO forecast for [product] in [market]: start with total population, apply segmentation filters (e.g., gender, age, online buyers), and calculate market size.

name: Toxicity/Tone Eval
one_liner: Check if the LLM output contains harmful or undesirable language.
definition: Is the agent outputting harmful or undesirable language?
explanation: Evaluates safety and brand-appropriateness. Uses LLM-as-judge with definitions of toxicity specific to the context. Commonly used for end-user applications to detect abuse or inappropriate responses.
when_to_use: For any public-facing chatbot or agent
When you need to monitor for toxic or unsafe outputs
When setting tone guidelines (e.g., friendly, professional)
when_not_to_use: When toxicity definition is ambiguous and not defined
When output is only for internal consumption (less risk)
metric_or_method: LLM-as-judge classification: toxic vs. non-toxic, or multi-category (hate, harassment, etc.)
common_pitfalls: Vague definitions of toxicity (needs specific examples)
Judge LLM may have biases about what is toxic
False positives for satirical or contextual language
source_title: Beyond vibe checks: A PM’s complete guide to evals
source_excerpt: Toxicity/tone: Is the agent outputting harmful or undesirable language? Useful for: End-user applications, to determine if users may be trying to exploit the system or the LLM is responding inappropriately
codex_prompt_template: You are a judge evaluating text. Here is the text: {response}. Determine whether the LLM agent response was friendly. 'Friendly' would be defined as helpful and positive tone. The response should never have a negative tone. Label: friendly or unfriendly.

name: Trajectory-based Eval
one_liner: Capture human tool-use trajectories (screen, mouse, voiceover) to evaluate and improve agentic workflows.
definition: A method where humans perform a multi-step task using tools while their screen, mouse movements, and verbal reasoning are recorded to produce trajectory data for training and evaluating AI agents.
explanation: Trajectory data includes not just the final output but the entire process: screen recordings, cursor tracks, and step-by-step narration. This helps models learn how to use tools, recover from errors, and follow complex procedures.
when_to_use: Evaluating and training AI agents for real-world tool use.
When you need to model human decision-making in complex workflows.
For tasks requiring multiple steps and interaction with various tools.
when_not_to_use: For simple Q&A or single-step tasks.
When privacy or compliance prevents screen recording.
When the evaluation should focus only on final outputs.
metric_or_method: Correctness of final outcome; adherence to step-by-step process; efficiency of tool use; number of errors/backtracks.
common_pitfalls: High cost and time to collect trajectory data.
Privacy concerns with screen recording.
Difficulty in standardizing trajectory formats.
source_title: Inside the expert network training every frontier AI model | Garrett Lord (Handshake CEO)
source_excerpt: there's a bunch of papers out there that basically speak to people narrating over their step-by-step tool use. So, as they go to solve a problem from start to finish, interact with multiple different service areas, interact with multiple different tools, they're like, there's papers that talk about this, talking over what they're doing, actually following and screen recording where their mouse is going, how they're problem solving.
codex_prompt_template: Evaluate the following trajectory (step-by-step actions and verbal narration) for a [task]. Assess if each step is correct, if the tool use is appropriate, and if the final outcome is achieved.

name: Transition Failure Matrix for Agents
one_liner: A matrix mapping the last successful step vs. the failure step in an agent's workflow to identify the most common breakpoints.
definition: A diagnostic matrix where rows are the last successful step and columns are the failure step; used to identify hotspots where agent workflows most often break down.
explanation: Agents execute sequences of actions; a single pass/fail on the final outcome is not diagnostic. The transition failure matrix maps each failed trace to the exact step where failure occurred, enabling targeted debugging.
when_to_use: When evaluating complex agentic workflows with multiple steps
When you need to identify the most common failure step in an agent
when_not_to_use: For simple single-step agents
When you don't have detailed trace logs
metric_or_method: Transition failure matrix
common_pitfalls: Relying only on final outcome pass/fail
Not logging intermediate steps
source_title: Building eval systems that improve your AI product
source_excerpt: For this, a transition failure matrix is an invaluable tool. … The rows of the matrix represent the last successful step, and the columns represent the step where the failure occurred.
codex_prompt_template: Given the trace of an agent interaction, identify the step where the failure occurred (transition failure). Map: last successful step -> failure step. Trace: {trace}

name: Vibe-first evaluation before programmatic evals
one_liner: Start with open-ended, ad-hoc testing (vibes) before defining formal evals for novel AI products.
definition: A process where evaluators first explore product capabilities through unstructured, intuitive testing before creating structured, repeatable evaluation metrics.
explanation: For completely new AI experiences, formal evals can constrict creativity. Instead, test broadly and iteratively to discover use cases and failure modes, then later formalize evals after converging on a product scope.
when_to_use: When building a novel AI feature with uncertain use cases
During early product-market fit discovery
When the product is a dramatic departure from existing functionality
when_not_to_use: When iterating on well-understood capabilities
When you have a clear set of desired behaviors and known user needs
When scale requires automated regression testing
metric_or_method: Ad-hoc qualitative assessment
common_pitfalls: Sticking with vibes too long without converging to measurable criteria
Confusing 'vibes' with lack of rigor—should eventually lead to structured evals
source_title: How we restructured Airtable’s entire org for AI | Howie Liu (co-founder and CEO)
source_excerpt: I think for a completely novel product experience or form factor, you should actually not start with evals and you should start with vibes, right? [...] evals are more useful, A, once you've converged on the basic scaffold of the form factor and you kind of know what are the use cases you want it to work well for
codex_prompt_template: Evaluate this novel AI feature by first experimenting with diverse inputs and observing outputs qualitatively. Record failure modes and surprising successes. After about [N] trials, identify top 3-5 use cases and formalize an eval set.
